shower.thought: We spend our whole lives learning how to be ourselves, and by the time we figure it out, we're supposed to be someone else for our kids.
The people who hurt us most are usually the ones we gave the most information about how to do it.
You can't remember most of your life, which means you're not actually living it—you're just experiencing it once and keeping highlights.
Nostalgia is just grief wearing a smile.
We treat our bodies like they're separate from us—feeding them, exercising them, medicating them—but they're the only part of us that's actually real.
The hardest part about being an adult isn't responsibility; it's realizing your parents were just making it up as they went too.
You probably have a best friend whose favorite person isn't you, and you'll never know because you'll never ask.
Time moves slower when you're paying attention, which is why childhood felt infinite and adulthood disappears.
We say we want to be heard, but most of the time we just want someone to stop talking long enough to let us be right.
The most productive thing you can do is nothing, but our culture has convinced us that's the same as failure.
You're the villain in someone's story right now, and you have no idea.
Boredom might be the only honest emotion—everything else is just you trying to convince yourself life means something.
We're all terrified of being forgotten, which is ironic because forgetting people is how we survive them.
Your parents are the only people on Earth who have loved you for no reason at all, and you'll spend your whole life trying to find that again.
Sleep is the closest thing humans have to dying, but we complain when we don't get enough of it.
The saddest part about moving on isn't losing the person—it's realizing they moved on without noticing you were trying.
We call them "bad memories" but they're usually the only ones we actually remember clearly.
You've already met the person who'll hurt you the most, and you probably considered them family.
Confidence is just being too busy to doubt yourself, but insecurity means you're paying attention.
The things we regret aren't usually what we did—they're the versions of ourselves we killed to do it.

motivational.thought: The cave you fear to enter holds the treasure you seek. — Joseph Campbell
Discipline is just choosing between what you want now and what you want most. — Unknown
You can't think your way into a new way of living. You have to live your way into a new way of thinking. — Unknown
Everything worthwhile costs something. The question is whether you're willing to pay it. — Unknown
Your excuses are someone else's opportunities. — Unknown
The smallest versions of your dreams are still dreams worth having. — Unknown
Comfort is the enemy of growth, and growth is the price of aliveness. — Unknown
You will never have all the information you need to make a decision. Decide anyway. — Unknown
What feels impossible today becomes your baseline tomorrow. — Unknown
The people who changed the world weren't waiting to feel ready. — Unknown
Your past doesn't qualify or disqualify your future unless you let it. — Unknown
The regret of not trying is worse than the pain of failing. — David Goggins
Momentum is built in the smallest moments, not the biggest ones. — Unknown
You don't rise to the level of your goals. You fall to the level of your systems. — Unknown
The only approval you need is the kind you give yourself. — Unknown
What you think about in the shower is what you need to do. — Unknown
Starting is a form of genius that most people never attempt. — Unknown
Your resistance to something is proportional to how much you need it. — Carl Jung
The life you're waiting for begins with decisions you make today. — Unknown
Healing isn't linear, and neither is progress. Both are still progress. — Unknown

darkjoke.thought:
I told my friend he was being paranoid about his girlfriend cheating.
He said, "You're right, I'm being ridiculous." I said, "No, I meant about it being paranoia."

My therapist asked why I never share my feelings.
I said, "Because nobody cares." She said, "I care." I said, "That'll be $200."
I'm not saying my family is dysfunctional, but our group chat has a restraining order.

My dad said he was proud of me for finally getting my life together.
I said, "Thanks." He said, "Don't thank me, thank your medication."

I asked my Roomba if it ever felt like it was just going in circles.
It said, "Have you met humans?"

My friend said he was cutting back on drinking for his health.
I said, "That's great." He said, "Yeah, I've switched to energy drinks." I said, "That's not better." He said, "It tastes like I'm dying faster, so at least it feels fresh."

I told my mom she was being too critical of my life choices.
She said, "You're right, I'm sorry." I said, "Really?" She said, "No, I was being sarcastic. You married a dentist named Kyle."

My boss asked if I had any questions about my performance review.
I said, "Yeah—how are you this delusional?"

I said goodbye to my friend like I meant it this time.
He said, "Are you finally moving away?" I said, "No, just saying goodbye to him emotionally."

My doctor said I should exercise more.
I said, "I do—I run away from my problems." He said, "That's not exercise, that's cardio." I said, "Exactly."

I told my coworker that nobody likes him.
He said, "That's not true." I said, "You're right, I'm sorry—I like you." He said, "Really?" I said, "No, I was seeing if that made you feel worse."

My therapist told me I have commitment issues.
I said, "Maybe." She said, "That's not comforting." I said, "Neither is $300 an hour, but here we are."

I asked my friend why he was still with someone who made him miserable.
He said, "Inertia." I said, "That's not a reason to stay in a relationship." He said, "Have you tried leaving? Inertia wins."

My parents said they wanted to be more involved in my adult life.
I sent them my therapy bills. They're no longer interested.

I told my friend he should stop catastrophizing.
He said, "You're right." I said, "See, that wasn't so bad." He said, "Give it time."

My barber asked how I wanted my hair cut.
I said, "Make me look successful." He said, "That's not a haircut, that's magic."

I said I was going through a phase.
My therapist said, "You've been saying that for five years." I said, "Long phase." She said, "That's not a phase, that's just your personality."

My friend said he was going to stop overthinking everything.
I said, "Good idea." He said, "But what if that's the wrong idea?" I said, "There it is."

I told my coworker her ideas were crazy.
She said, "At least I have ideas." I said, "Fair point, but they're still crazy." She said, "Better than no ideas and an air of judgment." I said, "That's two things I have."

My spouse said I never do anything spontaneous.
So I left without telling them. They didn't find it fun.
